title: Operations & Martech
tag: PM, Campaigns, Stakeholders, Go-Live, SEO & GEO
scan: What you will learn: The full operational envelope around a digital project — PM cadences, campaign phases, stakeholder risk patterns, go-live gates, and search readiness
Why it matters: Engineering quality is invisible at go-live if the operational layer fails; a flawless build ships to nobody if DNS, stakeholders, or SEO readiness are mismanaged
Stakeholder expectation management is a design discipline — the same risk patterns (scope creep, late arrivals, silent disagreements) recur on every large engagement
SEO readiness is a pre-launch gate, not a post-launch audit — crawl budget, canonicals, and redirect maps must be resolved before DNS cut-over, not after
GEO (Generative Engine Optimisation) is additive to SEO — structured data and answer-ready content blocks serve both traditional search rankings and AI citation engines simultaneously

blocks.html: of CODE is where strategy becomes operational reality. It's the cadence of work — how a campaign moves from idea to live, who signs off, what tools support each step. PMs and Delivery Leads own this layer; architects need to understand it well enough to know where engineering plugs into it.

items: ↳ Each gate is a named owner with a sign-off SLA · no gate skips · Live is the DNS cut-over, with TTL lowered 48h in advance

blocks.html: Campaign management requires an architect at the table. Campaign dates drive infrastructure scaling decisions — CDN pre-warming schedules, cache TTL reduction windows, autoscale thresholds, and load test timing are all campaign inputs. A PM who books a campaign launch without the architect knowing means the infrastructure isn't ready for the traffic spike that campaign will generate.

DEPT® field pattern — the "T-minus board": a shared Confluence page updated daily in the final two weeks, showing every open item by owner and date. Reduces status-meeting time by 60% because status is always current and visible. Every outstanding item has one owner; nothing is "team" or "TBD" on a T-minus board.

The most common go-live failure is DNS TTL not reduced 48 hours in advance. A TTL of 3600 on launch day means half your users see the old site for an hour after cut-over. Reduce TTL to 300 two days before the switch; restore it after cut-over is confirmed and stable.
Four disciplines hold a project together. They are mundane, repetitive, and the single biggest predictor of whether the team will finish — separate from anything about engineering quality.

cards.title: DSM · Daily Stand-up
cards.body: 15-minute daily ritual. Each person answers three questions — what I did yesterday, what I'll do today, what's blocking me. Purpose is surfacing blockers fast, not status reporting.

cards.title: Workflow management
cards.body: Where work lives across its lifecycle — backlog, in progress, review, blocked, done. Jira (mature, enterprise) and ProofHub (lighter, agency-friendly) are the common picks. The tool isn't the discipline; the workflow definition is.

cards.title: Governance
cards.body: Decision rights (who can approve what), escalation paths (when stuck, who breaks the tie), change control (how scope changes get reviewed), and status reporting cadence.

cards.title: RACI
cards.body: Responsible does the work · Accountable owns the outcome (one person only, always) · Consulted gives input before · Informed is told after. Fill it in for every key activity; hang the matrix where everyone can see.

blocks.html: Every campaign flows through the same five phases. The work products differ by industry; the phases don't.

cards.title: Plan
cards.body: Brief, audience, objectives, KPIs, budget, timeline, success criteria. If you can't write these on one page, you don't have a campaign yet.

cards.title: Build
cards.body: Creative production, content, landing pages, tech setup, QA, dry-run. The phase with the most opinions per square metre.

cards.title: Launch
cards.body: Go-live checklist, comms to stakeholders, monitoring setup. The point of no return.

cards.title: Measure
cards.body: Daily checks in week one, weekly thereafter; attribution and contribution analysis.

cards.title: Iterate
cards.body: Optimise the live campaign (A/B tests, audience refinement) and document learnings before the next plan starts.

blocks.html: The most undervalued discipline in delivery. A technically perfect project with mismanaged expectations is a failed project. A technically average project with managed expectations is a success.

items: Surface assumptions early — what stakeholders think they're getting vs what they're getting. The gap closes only when named out loud.
Define scope explicitly — in, out, stretch — and write it down. Verbal scope evaporates by week three.
Set the cadence — weekly status, escalation criteria, who hears what when. Predictable rhythm beats brilliant ad-hoc updates.
The triangle — time, cost, quality — pick two; the third gives. Make stakeholders make the choice.
Write decisions down — hallway conversations evaporate; written decisions hold the team accountable to themselves.
Show progress, not just problems — bring two options to status meetings, not one complaint.
Scope creep — every "small addition" compounds. The fourth small addition is the one that breaks the schedule.
Late stakeholders — people who arrive late always have opinions. Find them early; you don't get to ignore them.
Politics — power dynamics shape what gets prioritised. Name them privately, navigate them deliberately.
Silent disagreements — what isn't said in the meeting shows up on the deploy weekend.

blocks.html: Two distinct readiness disciplines depending on the platform. Each has its own playbook — covered in future deep-dives:

cards.title: AEM EDS readiness
cards.body: Edge Delivery Services projects carry their own pre-flight checklist — document-based authoring, edge cache strategy, performance benchmarks, content fragment migration. Detailed deep-dive to follow.

cards.title: Adobe Commerce readiness
cards.body: Commerce go-lives have their own discipline — catalog and PIM migration, payment and tax integrations, checkout load testing, fulfilment cutover. Detailed deep-dive to follow.

items: ↳ The point at this layer: name the readiness discipline that applies, schedule it in the plan, don't conflate it with general QA.

sections.title: SEO & GEO readiness and expectation setting

blocks.html: 5 · SEO & GEO readiness and expectation setting
Two adjacent disciplines, one mature, one brand-new. Both are easy to over-promise on and hard to deliver on.

columns.label: SEO · Search Engine Optimization
columns.body:
The established discipline — rankings on Google, Bing, etc. Three pillars:
  • Technical SEO — sitemaps, structured data, performance, mobile, crawlability.
  • Content SEO — keyword strategy, topical authority, depth.
  • Off-page SEO — backlinks, mentions, domain authority.

columns.label: GEO · Generative Engine Optimization
columns.body:
The new discipline — optimising for AI answer engines (ChatGPT, Perplexity, Claude, Google AI Overviews). Focus shifts:
  • From "rank" to "be cited" — appear in the AI's answer, with a source link back.
  • Levers — structured content with clear claims, source links, freshness signals, schema markup, brand mentions across the web.
  • Maturity — no playbook is fully proven yet. Frame as experiments, not commitments.

items: SEO results take 3–6 months minimum to materialise — set this up front, in writing, signed off.
GEO is too new for confident promises — frame work as experiments, measure citation rate and inclusion in AI answers, share learning as it accrues.
Define success before work starts — rankings? organic traffic? conversions? citations? Pick the metric, pick the target, pick the timeline.
Educate on what's controllable — technical hygiene and content are; algorithm changes and competitor moves aren't.

blocks.label: Architect's Review · Ops & Martech
items: Is there a documented workflow per work type, or does each team improvise?
Does RACI exist for the critical activities, with named people?
Is the campaign lifecycle managed end-to-end in one tool, or scattered across five?
Is stakeholder communication on a defined cadence, with named owners?
Is go-live readiness a checklist or a vibe?
Are SEO and GEO expectations realistic, documented, and signed off?
Where are the biggest workflow bottlenecks — and who owns fixing them?
